items.front: What is the mission of the traffic management system?
items.back: Balance air traffic demand with system capacity to ensure the maximum efficient utilization of the NAS. Per 11-1-1a.

items.front: Which tool must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to miles-in-trail initiatives?
items.back: TBFM (Time Based Flow Management). Per 11-1-1b.

items.front: When can TBFM be set aside in favor of MIT?
items.back: Only when TBFM is not feasible. The standard is maximum extent feasible, not equal standing. Per 11-1-1b.

items.front: What role does the ATCS play in the traffic management mission?
items.back: The ATCS is integral in the execution of the traffic management mission. Per 11-1-1c.

items.front: Where can complete details of TBM, traffic management initiatives, and programs be found?
items.back: FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration. Per note to 11-1-1.

items.front: Balance air traffic demand with system capacity to ensure the maximum efficient utilization of the NAS.
items.back: What is the mission of the traffic management system? Per 11-1-1a.

items.front: How often must the STMCIC ensure an operational briefing is conducted?
items.back: At least once during the day and evening shifts. Per 11-1-2a1.

items.front: Who are the minimum required participants in the STMCIC operational briefing?
items.back: STMCIC, OSMIC/CIC, and other interested personnel as designated by facility management. Per 11-1-2a1.

items.front: What topics should the STMCIC operational briefing include?
items.back: Meteorological conditions (present and forecasted), staffing, equipment status, runways in use, Airport Arrival Rate (AAR), TBM use, and Traffic Management Initiatives (TMIs) (present and anticipated). Per 11-1-2a1.

items.front: Who assumes responsibility for TMC duties when the TMC position is not staffed?
items.back: The STMCIC. Per 11-1-2a2.

items.front: What must the OS/CIC do regarding TBM operations and TMIs affecting their area?
items.back: Continuously review them and coordinate with TMU for extensions, revisions, or cancellations. Per 11-1-2b3.

items.front: What two things do TBM operations and TMIs NOT have priority over for personnel providing air traffic services?
items.back: 1. Separation of aircraft. 2. Procedural integrity of the sector. Per 11-1-2c1.

items.front: What is the airborne release window when departure scheduling or Call for Release is in effect?
items.back: From 2 minutes prior to 1 minute after the assigned time, unless otherwise coordinated. Per 11-1-2f3.

items.front: How must an ATCT obtain a departure release time when departure scheduling is in effect and the tower is equipped?
items.back: Use automation to obtain a departure release time from the TBM system. Per 11-1-2f2.

items.front: Distinguish the ARTCC TBFM duty from the ATCT TBFM duty.
items.back: ARTCCs must support TBFM operations AND monitor arrival flow for metering changes. ATCTs must monitor TBFM equipment, and when equipped with departure scheduling, use automation for departure release times and release within the prescribed window. Per 11-1-2d,f.

items.front: What additional duty do TRACONs have that ARTCCs do not, beyond TBFM support and arrival-flow monitoring?
items.back: Schedule internal departures in accordance with specific written procedures and agreements developed with overlying ARTCCs and adjacent facilities. Per 11-1-2e3.

items.front: What forms can coordination regarding TBM operations and TMIs take?
items.back: Verbal, electronic, or written. Per note to 11-1-2.

items.front: Which three parties must personnel providing air traffic services coordinate with for TMI extensions, revisions, or cancellations?
items.back: OS/CIC and TMU. Per 11-1-2c3.

items.front: During metering, where must TBFM schedule information be displayed?
items.back: On the situation display. Per 11-1-3a.

items.front: What is the standard TBFM metering-time compliance tolerance?
items.back: Within plus or minus 1 minute. Per 11-1-3b.

items.front: What should be done when DCT instability prevents accurate metering-time compliance for specific aircraft?
items.back: Use TMIs such as miles-in-trail (MIT) or minutes-in-trail (MINIT) between those aircraft to assist delay absorption until stability resumes. Per 11-1-3b1.

items.front: Can the plus-or-minus-1-minute metering standard be excepted, and if so, how?
items.back: Yes. An exception may be authorized for certain ARTCC sectors if explicitly defined in an appropriate facility directive. Per 11-1-3b2.

items.front: When metering-time compliance is not possible, who must you coordinate with?
items.back: OS/CIC, personnel providing traffic management services, and adjacent facilities/sectors as appropriate. Per 11-1-3c.

items.front: What factors does the note cite as affecting TBFM accuracy of generated metering times?
items.back: Vectoring outside TBFM route conformance boundaries (route recovery logic), certain trajectory ground speed calculations, and when TMU resequences a specific flight or flight list. Per note to 11-1-3.

items.front: What are the two foundational compliance steps during metering?
items.back: 1. Display TBFM schedule information on the situation display. 2. Comply with TBFM-generated metering times within plus or minus 1 minute. Per 11-1-3a-b.
